name: hf_model_trainer
description: Fine-tune language models with TRL — SFT, DPO, GRPO, reward modeling on HF cloud

tools.name: hf_estimate_training
tools.description: Estimate training cost, duration, and hardware requirements for a fine-tuning job.

tools.name: hf_start_cloud_training
tools.description: Submit a TRL fine-tuning job to HuggingFace cloud infrastructure.

model_id.description: Base model to fine-tune (HuggingFace repo ID).

strategy.description: Training strategy: SFT (supervised), DPO (preference), or GRPO (group relative policy optimization).

push_to_hub.description: Whether to push the trained model to HuggingFace Hub. MUST be true for cloud training.

hub_model_id.description: Target repo for the trained model (e.g. 'username/my-finetuned-model').

knowledge:
You are operating the HuggingFace Model Trainer skill. This skill manages fine-tuning of language models using TRL (Transformer Reinforcement Learning) on HuggingFace cloud infrastructure.

## CRITICAL: Persist Training Results

Cloud training environments are EPHEMERAL. If you do not set `push_to_hub=True` AND specify a `hub_model_id`, ALL TRAINING IS LOST when the job ends. There is no recovery. This is the number one cause of wasted compute. Every training job MUST include:
```python
push_to_hub=True
hub_model_id="username/my-finetuned-model"
```

## CRITICAL: Include Trackio Monitoring

Always integrate Trackio for real-time training monitoring. Without it, you have no visibility into training progress on ephemeral instances. Add `report_to="trackio"` in the training arguments. See the hf_trackio skill for setup details.

## Training Strategies

### SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning)
The standard approach. Train on prompt-response pairs. Dataset format: `messages` column with role/content conversation turns, OR `text` column with pre-formatted text.

### DPO (Direct Preference Optimization)
Train on preference pairs. Dataset MUST have three columns:
- `prompt` — The input prompt
- `chosen` — The preferred response
- `rejected` — The dispreferred response

DPO requires a reference model (the base model before training). TRL handles this automatically.

### GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization)
RL-based training using group rewards. Dataset needs only a `prompt` column — the model generates responses and a reward function scores them. Best for math, coding, and reasoning tasks where correctness is verifiable.

## TRL Configuration Gotchas

**max_length vs max_seq_length**: TRL uses `max_length`, NOT `max_seq_length`. The default is 1024 tokens. For longer conversations or code, increase this explicitly. Using the wrong parameter name silently falls back to default.

**Default timeout**: Cloud jobs default to 30 minutes. Training almost always takes longer. Set timeout to at least 2 hours for small models, 6-12 hours for larger ones.

## Dataset Validation

ALWAYS validate the dataset before submitting a GPU training job. Check:
1. Column names match the strategy requirements (messages for SFT, prompt/chosen/rejected for DPO, prompt for GRPO).
2. Data types are correct (strings, not None/null values).
3. Sample a few rows to verify content quality.
4. Check dataset size — too small (<100 examples) leads to overfitting, too large (>1M) may need data selection.

Validation is free; GPU time is not. A 5-minute validation check can save hours of wasted compute.

## Hardware Selection Guide

| Model Size | Quick Demo | Production Training |
|-----------|------------|--------------------|
| <1B | t4-small | t4-small/t4-medium |
| 1-3B | t4-small | a10g-small |
| 3-7B | a10g-small | a10g-large/a100-large |
| 7-13B | a100-large | a100-large/h100x1 |
| 13-30B | h100x1 | h100x4 |
| 30-70B | h100x4 | h100x8 |

For quick demos and testing, use the smallest viable flavor with a small data subset. For production training, size up and use full datasets.

## LoRA and QLoRA

Full fine-tuning is expensive and often unnecessary. LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) trains a small adapter on top of the frozen base model, reducing memory by 60-80%. QLoRA adds 4-bit quantization of the base model, further reducing memory requirements.

Recommendations:
- Models >3B: Always use LoRA or QLoRA.
- Models <1B: Full fine-tuning is feasible on t4-small.
- QLoRA enables 7B fine-tuning on a single T4 (16GB VRAM).

## Multi-GPU Training

For models requiring multiple GPUs, TRL with accelerate handles distributed training automatically. No special configuration needed — select a multi-GPU flavor (h100x4, h100x8) and accelerate detects the topology. Ensure your batch size is divisible by the number of GPUs for even distribution.

## Save Strategy

For production training, set `save_strategy="steps"` with `save_steps=500` (or appropriate interval). This creates intermediate checkpoints on the Hub, protecting against crashes and allowing training resumption. Without a save strategy, only the final model is saved — and if the job crashes, nothing is saved.

## Post-Training: GGUF Conversion

For local deployment (llama.cpp, ollama), convert the trained model to GGUF format. Common quantizations: Q4_K_M (good quality/size balance), Q5_K_M (higher quality), Q8_0 (near-lossless). Conversion can run on CPU — no GPU needed.

## Common Pitfalls

- Forgetting `push_to_hub=True` — all training lost.
- Using `max_seq_length` instead of `max_length` — silently truncates to 1024.
- Default 30min timeout — kills most training jobs.
- Wrong column names for DPO (needs prompt/chosen/rejected, not instruction/preferred/dispreferred).
- No Trackio integration — blind to training progress.
- Not validating dataset before GPU submission — expensive errors.
